questions.text: Which sentence uses the correct present simple form?

options.text: She go to school every day.
She goes to school every day.
She going to school every day.
She gone to school every day.

questions.explanation: In present simple, third-person singular (he/she/it) requires adding -s or -es to the verb. 'Go' becomes 'goes'.

questions.text: The sentence 'He play tennis on Sundays.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! Third-person singular requires 'plays', not 'play'. The correct sentence is: 'He plays tennis on Sundays.'

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'My sister do not like coffee.'
questions.explanation: With third-person singular subjects (he/she/it), use 'does not' (doesn't) instead of 'do not' (don't).

questions.text: Choose the correct article to complete the sentence: '___ apple a day keeps the doctor away.'
questions.explanation: Use 'an' before words that begin with a vowel sound. 'Apple' starts with the vowel sound /æ/, so we use 'an'.

questions.text: The sentence 'I saw a elephant at the zoo.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! 'Elephant' begins with a vowel sound, so we must use 'an', not 'a'. The correct sentence is: 'I saw an elephant at the zoo.'

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'She is a honest person.'
questions.explanation: Although 'honest' starts with the letter 'h', the 'h' is silent, so the word begins with a vowel sound /ɒ/. Use 'an' before vowel sounds.

questions.text: Which preposition correctly completes: 'The meeting is ___ Monday morning.'
questions.explanation: Use 'on' with days of the week and specific dates (on Monday, on 5th July). Use 'in' for months/years, and 'at' for specific times.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'I have lived here since three years.'
questions.explanation: Use 'for' with a period/duration of time (for three years, for a week). Use 'since' with a specific point in time (since 2020, since Monday).

questions.text: What is the correct plural form of 'child'?
questions.explanation: 'Child' is an irregular noun. Its plural is 'children', not 'childs'. Other irregular plurals include: man→men, woman→women, tooth→teeth, foot→feet.
Incorrect! Nouns ending in -o (preceded by a consonant) form their plural with -es: tomato→tomatoes, potato→potatoes. However, some exceptions exist (e.g., photos, pianos).
